title: Dissect and Prune: Enhancing Robustness in AI-Generated Image Detection
abstract: While existing AI-generated image detectors report high performance, we identify that this is largely driven by a critical *prediction asymmetry*: a bias toward the real class that severely limits sensitivity to generated content, especially under standard post-processing operations such as compression and resizing. We hypothesize that this stems from the model's reliance on spurious features, distracting signals that obscure true generative artifacts. To address this, we propose DEAR (Dissect and Prune), which leverages inpainted images to identify and prune these interfering components. Specifically, we find that features strongly aligned to either inpainted or non-inpainted regions are less robust to post-processing. By measuring the alignment between channel activations and inpaint masks, DEAR removes features at both extremes, retaining only those that capture genuine generative artifacts. Experimental results demonstrate that our approach significantly enhances robustness against unseen generators and post-processing, effectively mitigating the prediction asymmetry. Our code is available at https://github.com/dahyedahye/dear.
decision_comment: The authors have addressed all reviewers’ concerns, and all three reviewers recommend acceptance; therefore, there is a clear decision to accept the paper.

reviews.summary:
This paper investigates the robustness of AI-generated image detectors and identifies a prediction asymmetry issue: while these detectors achieve high accuracy on authentic images, they often fail to reliably identify generated ones, particularly following post-processing operations. The authors attribute this limitation to reliance on non-robust features instead of leveraging intrinsic generative artifacts.
To mitigate this problem, the proposed framework DEAR analyzes feature channels derived from inpainted images by quantifying their alignment with both synthetic and authentic regions using a novel Robust Alignment Discrepancy (RAD) metric. Channels exhibiting extreme alignment discrepancies are pruned to retain only robust features within the classifier's input space. Subsequently, classification models are fine-tuned over this refined feature representation. Experimental evaluations conducted across diverse generative models, post-processing scenarios, and real-world benchmarks demonstrate that DEAR significantly enhances detection robustness compared to state-of-the-art methods.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
## Strengths
This work addresses a significant challenge in detecting AI-generated images, specifically focusing on issues related to prediction asymmetry and limited robustness when subjected to post-processing techniques or novel generative models. The proposed DEAR framework demonstrates simplicity in design while offering seamless integration into existing detection systems without necessitating retraining of backbone networks. Leveraging inpainting for feature analysis introduces an innovative approach that facilitates understanding of feature behaviors within detection tasks. Experimental evaluations are extensive, encompassing diverse generative models, various post-processing scenarios, and real-world benchmarks. Results consistently exhibit performance improvements over competitive baseline methods.
## Weaknesses
Certain key assertions made by the authors—particularly the interpretation that contemporary detectors predominantly depend on spurious correlations—are insufficiently substantiated through rigorous empirical evidence and may be overstated. While the method relies heavily on diagnostic data derived from inpainting processes, there is inadequate investigation into whether compositing operations inadvertently introduce artifacts capable of skewing results. Furthermore, although the pruning strategy shows promise based on empirical evaluation with CNN-based architectures, its applicability across alternative model types remains uncertain due to limited theoretical justification provided within this study. Overall, while practical advancements are evident, conceptual contributions remain incremental with restricted theoretical grounding.
reviews.key_questions_for_authors:
1. The paper attributes the performance drop after post-processing to detectors learning spurious correlations, but the experiments only show reduced robustness under distribution shift. This does not necessarily imply the presence of spurious correlations. Could the authors clarify why the observed failure should be interpreted as evidence of spurious features rather than limited generalization to post-processed data?

2. In lines 17–20, the paper hypothesizes that prediction asymmetry is caused by the model relying on spurious features that obscure true generative artifacts. This is a key assumption of the work, but the paper does not provide sufficient evidence to support this causal claim. Could the authors provide stronger analysis to justify that the identified features are indeed spurious and not simply sensitive but valid cues?
3. The method uses inpainted images to separate real and generated regions, but the compositing process may introduce boundary artifacts even with mask smoothing. Such artifacts could be exploited by the detector and affect the feature alignment analysis. Could the authors clarify how they ensure that the inpainting boundary does not introduce unintended cues?
4. The pruning mask is computed using RAD scores derived from the inpainting-based diagnostic dataset, which may depend on the specific inpainting model and mask generation strategy. Could the authors discuss whether the selected channels remain consistent when different inpainting methods or mask settings are used?
5. The bilateral pruning strategy is motivated by the observation that channels at both extremes of the RAD distribution are less robust, but this is shown only for a limited set of CNN-based detectors. Could the authors comment on whether this observation holds for other architectures such as ViT-based or CLIP-based detectors?

reviews.summary:
This paper addresses a critical flaw in existing AI-generated image (AIGI) detectors: despite reporting high overall accuracy, these models are fundamentally biased toward classifying images as real. The authors call this "prediction asymmetry" — detectors maintain near-perfect accuracy on real images while failing badly on AI-generated ones, especially after common post-processing like JPEG compression or resizing. For instance, NPR's Fake Accuracy collapses from 95.9% to just 12.2% after post-processing, effectively making it a trivial classifier that defaults to predicting "real."

The authors attribute this failure to spurious correlations: detectors latch onto dataset-specific compression artifacts as signals of "realness," while overfitting to fragile generator-specific fingerprints for "fakeness" — neither of which survives real-world perturbations. To fix this, they propose DEAR (Dissect and Prune), a three-stage framework. It first uses inpainted images (real photos with AI-generated regions inserted) as diagnostic probes to score each feature channel's alignment with generated vs. real content via a Regional Activation Discrepancy (RAD) metric. Channels at both extremes of this spectrum are then pruned, and the final classifier is retrained on the leaner, more robust feature space.

DEAR's key insight is that pruning must be bilateral — removing both real-biased and fake-biased extreme features — since both types are fragile under perturbation. Applied to two existing detectors (Corvi and Rajan), DEAR achieves strong Fake Accuracy across 9 generative models and maintains robust performance after post-processing, far outperforming baselines. It also generalizes well to real-world benchmarks containing images scraped from social media, confirming that the robustness gains hold beyond controlled lab settings.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths
From a soundness perspective, the paper makes a technically credible contribution. The proposed RAD metric is a well-motivated adaptation of IoU-based Network Dissection to a binary spatial provenance setting, and its density-based formulation — inspired by the Chan-Vese functional — ensures invariance to mask area, which is a non-trivial design choice that prevents confounding between mask size and channel alignment scores. The experimental protocol is rigorous: evaluation across nine generative architectures spanning diffusion models, latent consistency models, and commercial systems, combined with three in-the-wild benchmarks, provides convincing evidence that the observed gains are not artifacts of a narrow test distribution. Critically, the authors correctly identify that aggregate metrics such as AUC are insufficient for characterizing detector behavior under class-conditional distribution shift, and they decompose performance into R.Acc and F.Acc throughout — a methodologically sound decision that should be standard practice in this subfield but rarely is. On presentation, the paper is well-organized and the argumentation is precise. The causal chain from spurious correlation to prediction asymmetry to bilateral pruning is logically tight, and Figure 4 — which empirically demonstrates the inverted-U relationship between RAD magnitude and perturbation robustness — is the paper's most intellectually compelling visualization, directly motivating the bilateral rather than unilateral pruning strategy. The positioning relative to Stay Positive is handled with appropriate nuance: the authors correctly characterize that method as operating without channel-level interpretability, and they demonstrate empirically that ignoring the positive RAD extreme leaves a meaningful vulnerability unaddressed. The significance of the work extends beyond the specific method: by formally defining prediction asymmetry and demonstrating that it is systematically masked by standard reporting conventions, the paper delivers a diagnostic contribution that is likely to influence how the AIGI detection community designs and reports evaluations going forward. On originality, the repurposing of inpainted images — typically used for data augmentation — as structured interpretability probes with known spatial ground truth is an elegant and non-obvious insight. The bilateral pruning criterion, derived empirically from the RAD-robustness relationship rather than assumed a priori, reflects genuine analytical care rather than heuristic engineering.

Weaknesses
On soundness, the most substantive concern is the epistemological status of the RAD computation itself. The diagnostic inpainting is performed exclusively with Stable Diffusion 1.5, a model whose decoder artifacts and spectral characteristics are well-represented in the training distributions of both Corvi and Rajan. It is therefore unclear whether the RAD scores capture a generator-agnostic notion of "generative artifact alignment" or whether they are implicitly biased toward SD 1.5-specific artifact signatures. If the latter, the pruning decisions may not generalize to detectors trained on or evaluated against architecturally distinct generators — a concern that is particularly acute given that the paper's stated goal is cross-generator robustness. A controlled ablation varying the inpainting model used for diagnosis would substantially strengthen this claim. Furthermore, the bilateral pruning threshold α is selected empirically with no theoretical grounding for its transferability across backbone architectures or training regimes; the sensitivity analysis in the appendix reveals non-trivial performance variation, yet the paper offers no principled criterion for threshold selection beyond grid search. On presentation, the relegation of key implementation details — including mask sampling distribution, Gaussian blur parameters at inpaint boundaries, and the classifier retraining schedule — to the appendix impedes reproducibility assessment from the main text. For a method whose correctness depends sensitively on the quality of the inpainted diagnostic data, these details warrant more prominent treatment. Regarding significance, DEAR's applicability is currently demonstrated exclusively on ResNet-50 backbones, which limits the immediate generalizability of the framework. CLIP-based and ViT-based detectors, which constitute a substantial and growing proportion of the state-of-the-art, rely on global attention mechanisms rather than spatially localized convolutional feature maps — the foundational assumption underlying RAD's spatial averaging formulation. Whether an analogous dissection procedure can be defined for attention heads or patch-level tokens is a non-trivial open question that the paper does not address. On originality, while the combination of Network Dissection, inpainting, and feature pruning is well-motivated, each constituent is a well-established technique, and the paper would benefit from a more rigorous theoretical treatment of why the bilateral pruning of extreme RAD channels is necessary and sufficient for suppressing spurious correlations — rather than relying solely on the empirical correlation shown in Figure 4. A formal connection to the robustness literature, for instance through the lens of shortcut learning or invariant risk minimization, would considerably elevate the conceptual depth of the contribution.
reviews.key_questions_for_authors:
1. Generalizability of SD 1.5-derived RAD scores to architecturally distinct generators.
The diagnostic inpainting pipeline relies exclusively on Stable Diffusion 1.5, whose decoder artifacts and spectral signatures are plausibly well-represented in the training distributions of both Corvi and Rajan. This raises the question of whether the resulting RAD scores capture a generator-agnostic notion of generative artifact alignment, or whether they are implicitly biased toward SD 1.5-specific characteristics. Concretely: if the diagnostic inpainting were performed with an architecturally distinct model — such as FLUX or a GAN-based inpainter — would the resulting channel rankings and pruning decisions differ substantially? 

2. Principled selection of the bilateral pruning threshold α.
The current framework selects the pruning percentile α via empirical grid search, and the sensitivity analysis in the appendix reveals non-trivial performance variation across values. As the method stands, practitioners applying DEAR to a new detector would have no principled criterion for threshold selection without access to a labeled validation set — which may not always be available in deployment settings. Could the authors clarify whether a theoretically motivated or data-driven criterion for α selection exists, or whether the RAD distribution itself (e.g., its kurtosis or the sharpness of the robustness-alignment curve) provides a reliable proxy for identifying the appropriate pruning range? 

3. Necessity of bilateral pruning versus targeted unilateral pruning.
The paper argues compellingly that both real-aligned (negative RAD extreme) and fake-aligned (positive RAD extreme) channels must be pruned, and Figure 4 provides empirical support for the inverted-U robustness-alignment relationship. However, the paper does not present an ablation that isolates the contribution of each pruning direction independently — i.e., pruning only the negative extreme versus only the positive extreme versus both — across the full evaluation suite. The comparison against Rajan+ (which implicitly suppresses negative-RAD channels via non-negative weight constraints) provides partial evidence, but a controlled within-DEAR ablation separating the two pruning directions would more cleanly establish the necessity of bilaterality.
reviews.limitations:
No

Regarding limitations, the paper acknowledges the trade-off between Real Accuracy and Fake Accuracy introduced by pruning, and briefly notes sensitivity to the pruning threshold α. However, several substantive limitations identified in this review — most notably the restriction to ResNet-50 backbones, the reliance on a single inpainting model (SD 1.5) for diagnosis, and the absence of a principled threshold selection criterion — are not explicitly discussed in a dedicated limitations section. A transparent, consolidated treatment of these constraints would strengthen the paper's credibility and help practitioners assess the conditions under which DEAR can be reliably deployed.

reviews.summary: This paper identifies prediction asymmetry as a key limitation of existing AI-generated image detectors. To address this issue, the authors propose DEAR, which constructs masked diagnostic samples via inpainting and computes the mean activation difference (RAD) between generated regions and background regions for each channel. Based on this diagnostic signal, the method performs bilateral channel pruning, freezes the backbone network, and retrains only the linear classification head, thereby improving detector robustness.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths
a) The method is novel in that it uses inpainting mask-guided RAD to analyze channel responses and identify feature channels that remain more stable under post-processing perturbations, thereby improving detector robustness.
b) The method can be used as a post-hoc enhancement module for multiple baseline detectors and yields consistent performance gains across diverse generators and post-processing settings, supporting the effectiveness and transferability of the proposed strategy.

Weaknesses
a) A central operation in the paper is to remove the two extreme groups of channels ranked by RAD and retain only the middle portion, based on the claim that “RAD-extreme channels are less robust” (Fig. 4). However, greater robustness does not necessarily imply that the retained channels more faithfully capture generation artifacts. The channels in the middle RAD range may simply be less sensitive to regional variations, exhibit smoother responses, or be biased toward global texture statistics, and are therefore naturally more stable under compression. As a result, their robustness alone does not guarantee that they encode highly discriminative forgery artifacts.
b) While the reported results indicate promising performance gains, additional ablation studies are still needed to strengthen the empirical validation. In particular, it would be useful to compare DEAR against (1) a random channel-masking baseline that removes the same number of channels, and (2) one-sided pruning strategies (i.e., pruning only the extremely negative RAD channels or only the extremely positive RAD channels) versus the proposed two-sided pruning. These experiments would provide stronger evidence for the effectiveness of the method and help clarify the contribution of its core design choices.
c) After switching to the inpainting model, are the RAD ranking and performance stable?

These experiments would provide stronger evidence for the effectiveness of the method and help clarify the contribution of its core design choices.
